If you can remove the people who are just bouncing and browsing and not going down your page, you can improve your retargeting, you can improve your ROAS, your CPA, et cetera. Because here's the thing, not everyone that goes to your website should be retargeting. It's really important to understand that. So if you take a physical example, if you go into a shopping mall and then you walk into a store, you have a quick look around and walk out, you're not really someone that a salesperson wants to try and bring back in. If, for example, you've walked into the store and you've walked into a few different sections or aisles and you spent, let's say, 30 seconds, 45 seconds walking around, and then you're about to walk out, that's the point that salesperson should stop you and say, hey, can I help you with anything? That's the way to look at retargeting ads. So in my case, if you scroll to 25%, I want my salesperson to stop you and say, hey, is there anything I can do for you? That's what my retargeting ad does. ## [15:03 - 18:06]

because I'm not interested in other URLs. I can pull that URL out, for example, I can copy it, I can paste it and then drop that in and say only fire it on this. And then I could call it scroll 25, CBO cookbook page, whatever. So I know it's exactly that one. So there's different ways of creating that event. So that's really cool. The second thing is you can also create a custom audience and look likes from this as well. So I could create a custom audience that says people that have scrolled to that event in 30 days. You can also look at frequencies as well, which is pretty cool. So if someone scrolled to 25 and been on that page twice, then maybe they're a higher value customer. You can do that on many different events as well. So you could say people that hit the purchase page twice in the last 30 days or people that have added to cart more than twice in 15 days. So when it comes to building retargeting audiences, for example, you don't just have to look at the linear path of, okay, someone's added to cart, so I'm gonna retarget them with my best ads. You could segment that further, especially if you've got a lot of traffic and say if someone's added to cart twice in the last seven days, then I'm gonna give them this because they're more interested or maybe the cart value's gonna be higher because they're looking at multiple products. Or you could say, I wanna create a custom audience of people that have, I think this goes up to 180, yeah. So if you say people that purchased more than X times in the last 180 days or last 90 days, they could be high value customers. It might not be the case that you wanna target them through a custom audience, but maybe you create a lookalike of them. So some people will do it through their CRM data, so they'll look at emails and say, let's pull a list of people that bought more than X times or spent more than this much money or whatever it is. But you can also do it through the Pixel and I recommend doing it through both. And the reason is if you're just using emails to create your lifetime value customers, high value customers, et cetera, not everyone's email will match up with their Facebook profile. And I would conversely also say, don't just use the Pixel because sometimes the Pixel doesn't track so. Absolutely use both where you can. So if one of your cohorts is people that bought more than two times in the last 90 days and you wanna create a lookalike from that, create it in the Pixel and also creating your email data and test those and see which works. You can combine them together, for example, as well. So hopefully that gives you an insight into kind of how powerful the Pixel can be and how to use that. It doesn't matter what your product is, your niche, what you're selling, what your goal is. The Pixel is so powerful if you can set it up in the right way. At the very least, if you've got long form sales pages or you've got a product page that's fairly long, just use the scroll extension and start getting some data into how people are scrolling, use that for retargeting for customer audiences and lookalikes and you'll see far better data come through.

## [18:06 - 19:35]

And that's gonna be only limited by your imagination. So have a think about what makes sense for you. Scroll is one of my preferences. Obviously it's not full proof. Some people do scroll fast. Same goes with video remarketing as well. So if you're running a video ad, you can retarget people based on 25% view, 50, et cetera. Doesn't mean they've watched 25% of the video, just means they've hit that marker. And the same goes for your landing page. So it doesn't mean that if someone hits this 25% mark, they've actually slowly read that page. But here's the thing, if I then install time on page and I combine the two together, now we get an idea of what's going on. So if I say, I want people that scroll to 25% and have spent at least 30 seconds on the page, because I could set that up as a custom event as well, now I'm building up a better picture. So this is not someone who's just quickly scrolled the page. This is someone who's actually taken the time to read the content, especially when I go down to scroll 75, scroll 100. If I add a time element to this, now I can see people who are actually engaged. So if I retarget them, create look-alikes from that, the quality is going to be higher. So the advanced side of pixel management, not going to be for everyone, but if you want to get to a kind of e-cow, even better results and better performance, is definitely worth thinking about what you can do to enhance that pixel data and really enhance your results as well.
